metadata.seed: I made a mistake at work by not calling a placement for a resident and now they are unable to leave in time and my boss is upset.

patient.query: I made a mistake at work by not calling a placement for a resident and now they are unable to leave in time and my boss is upset.

automatic_thoughts: I should have done better
I'm letting everyone down
My boss will be angry with me

entailment: Delayed healthcare provision resulting in neglect (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of responsibility and worry about the resident’s situation and your boss’s reaction. It can be tough to see how a single oversight can ripple out. What’s been on your mind about how to move forward from here?

patient.query: Yeah, I've been thinking a lot about it. It just feels like every little mistake is magnified, you know? I keep replaying that moment in my head, worried that my boss will see me as unreliable. I just want to do a good job, but sometimes I feel like I'm on the edge of messing it all up.

automatic_thoughts: every little mistake is magnified
worried that my boss will see me as unreliable
I feel like I'm on the edge of messing it all up

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Forgets what was going to do (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure about how your boss perceives you. What evidence do you have that a single mistake will change their view of you? How might you see the situation if you considered other possible interpretations?

patient.query: I guess I just feel like I've built up this image of being competent, and any slip-up makes me think my boss will lose faith in me. It's like I can't shake that feeling that I'm almost an imposter, that one mistake will expose me. But, maybe you're right. I mean, it’s hard to see things differently when I’m so wrapped up in my own anxiety.

automatic_thoughts: my boss will lose faith in me
one mistake will expose me
I'm almost an imposter

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of losing your boss’s confidence is really weighing on you. What evidence do you have that a single mistake would lead to that outcome? Have there been times when a slip‑up didn’t change how your boss views you?

patient.query: Yeah, it does weigh on me, a lot. I mean, I can't think of any specific examples right now, but I know there have been other times when I’ve made mistakes and things turned out okay. But then I get this nagging thought that maybe this time is different, like maybe this is the mistake that changes everything. It's exhausting to think about.

automatic_thoughts: maybe this time is different
this is the mistake that changes everything

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to carry that nagging thought that this mistake will be different from the others. What evidence do you have that this one will actually change everything? You’ve mentioned that past mistakes ended up okay—how might that perspective help you view this situation now?

patient.query: I suppose I don’t really have hard evidence that this one will change everything. It’s just that voice in my head that gets louder when I'm stressed. It feels so real in the moment, but I guess looking back, I’ve survived those other mistakes. I just wish I could have the same confidence now. Instead, I’m stuck feeling anxious and defensive, and it makes it really hard to focus on anything else.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have hard evidence that this will change everything
That voice in my head gets louder when I'm stressed
It feels so real in the moment
I wish I could have the same confidence now
behaviors: hard to focus on anything else

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the internal voice gets louder when you’re stressed, and that makes you feel stuck and anxious. What evidence do you have that this situation will change everything? And what might be a small step that could help you feel a bit more confident right now?

patient.query: I don’t really have any solid evidence, honestly. It’s more like a fear that creeps in when I feel overwhelmed. I guess it’s just the pressure I put on myself to be perfect. A small step... maybe I could try talking to a coworker, get their perspective on the situation? But even that feels really daunting right now, like I might open myself up to criticism. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have solid evidence
I might open myself up to criticism

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to be perfect is really weighing on you. What evidence do you have that speaking up will lead to criticism? How might you test that idea in a small, low‑risk way?

patient.query: Yeah, the pressure is definitely heavy. I just keep imagining that if I ask for help or even just share what I'm feeling, they'll think I'm not capable. It’s hard to shake those thoughts. Testing it out seems scary, though. I guess I could try just talking to a friend at work about something minor first, but what if they don't respond well? I keep worrying that they'll see me in a different light. It makes me second-guess even what feels like a small step.

automatic_thoughts: they'll think I'm not capable
testing it out seems scary
what if they don't respond well?
they'll see me in a different light
second-guessing small steps

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that pressure feels and how it keeps you stuck in that loop of “not being capable.” What evidence do you have that people will actually judge you that way if you share? Could there be other ways to interpret their reactions that might not be as negative as you expect?

patient.query: I don’t know… I guess I just jump to conclusions in my head. It's like I always assume they’ll see me as weak or incapable, even if that’s not what they actually think. Maybe sometimes they might be understanding? But in those moments, all I can feel is that dread, like I'm being exposed. It’s just hard to see things differently, I guess. I wish I could trust that they'd be supportive instead of judging, but I get wrapped up in my fears.

automatic_thoughts: I always assume they’ll see me as weak or incapable
They might be judging me
I wish I could trust that they'd be supportive

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response: I’m curious—have you noticed any particular moments where someone’s words or actions made you feel judged? What other explanations could there be for those moments, besides a negative assessment of you? Often people act out of their own worries rather than a direct judgment of us. How might you try to test that idea in a small, low‑stakes situation?

patient.query: I think there have been moments when a coworker just seemed distant or didn't respond overly enthusiastic to my ideas, and my mind translated that into them thinking I'm not good enough. But you're right; maybe they were just having a bad day or dealing with their own stuff. I guess I could try sharing something light-hearted, like a funny story or a casual update, and see how they respond. It feels a bit less risky. But there's still that nagging thought that I could get it all wrong

automatic_thoughts: they think I'm not good enough
I could get it all wrong

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re already noticing that there might be other explanations for their reaction. What evidence do you have that they’re judging you, and what evidence might suggest they’re just preoccupied? How do you think a light‑hearted story could shift the conversation, and what would you hope to learn from their response?

patient.query: It’s true, I don’t really have solid proof they’re judging me. Most of the time, I read too much into things. Maybe they were just busy or caught up in their own heads. I guess sharing something light-hearted could lighten the mood, and I’d hope it opens up a different kind of connection. If they respond positively, maybe that would help me feel a bit more at ease. But there's still that little voice saying it’s too risky; it feels vulnerable, like I

automatic_thoughts: I don’t really have solid proof they’re judging me
I read too much into things
Maybe they were just busy or caught up in their own heads
It’s too risky
It feels vulnerable

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re weighing the potential benefits against the sense of vulnerability. What would it look like if the sharing went well? And what might happen if it doesn’t? How do you think you could manage that feeling of risk?
